The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 1 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 1 of 32
Bob Bly: 	So last time we said the premise of our series was to tell new
writers not only how to make a lot of money, but how to find all
the clients they’ll ever need, which is really the key to making a lot
of money. And we covered some of that, but Clayton, you have
additional tips to share on that topic?
Clayton Makepeace: 	A few, yeah. I don’t think we really got to all of them last time.
Some might be a little bit repetitive, but I just wanna underscore
the importance of samples, whether they be spec assignments that
you’ve completed, or actual samples that you’ve done. I had a
secretary just throw away 15 years of samples. And she’s still
alive, but it was an enormous loss to me. Fortunately, I’m at the
point in my career where my clients have more of my samples than
I did anyway, so I’m in okay shape there. But samples are solid
gold to a writer.
Sending them out to anybody and everybody that you could
possibly want to work for with a hard-hitting cover letter is how I
got started, and even though my samples were in another area. I
was wanting to write for newsletters, and all my samples were
about fundraising or water heaters or a bakery in Beverly Hills.
They could see the strength of the copy, and I got assignments as a
result. So that’s a real big key.
Bob Bly: 	So that’s an important point. You’re saying obviously it’s better if
the samples can be in the same area, but if they’re not, you can get
an assignment from a client in Product Area A even if your
samples are in Products B, C and D and you have no experience in
A. If they’re good samples.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Absolutely. If the copy’s strong, they’ll be able to spot it right off
the bat. Quite frankly, outside of the information publishing
industry, if you’re wanting to write for other kinds of companies,
they’re used to seeing samples from advertising companies and
copywriters that have nothing to do with their specific niche. And
so it seems quite natural for them to be reviewing that kind of
material. And in our industry, in newsletters, books and so forth,
in the financial and health areas, our clients are savvy enough to be
able to spot great copy when they see it. And they understand that
younger writers and beginning writers haven’t had the opportunity
to write in their industry before, and they’re highly motivated to
WARNING:
The following transcript is raw, uncut, and may contain grammatical errors.

-- 1 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 2 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 2 of 32
find the next hot copywriter, so their minds will be open to this
kind of thing.
Bob Bly: 	Having said that, once you have a sample – let’s say you do have a
sample in the client’s industry or product. Is that better to send?
What’s the best sample to send?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Obviously it would be samples that are closest to his area of
interest.
Bob Bly: 	Okay.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Because that way you begin developing your niche. I’ve required
that all of my clients send me a FedEx with 25 samples of every
package that I do.
Bob Bly: 	Is that in your verbal or written agreement with them?
Clayton Makepeace: 	It’s just verbal, but the other thing is I have one of these great new
handy-dandy $299.00 does-everything-but-wash-your-socks fax
machines/copiers/scanners sitting on my desktop. Only cost a
couple hundred dollars, and I can scan and make .PDFs of my
packages. In some cases, clients do, and that gives you an
inexhaustible supply of samples that you can send out instantly to
anyone in the country. So that’s really important. I think another
key is the networking key. We touched on it last time and in the
total package also. We talked about how Parris Lampropolis took
samples that were completely unrelated to the newsletter industry,
along with a hot cover letter, and walked into a conference where
there were clients who were looking for copywriters and walked
away with several assignments, tens of thousands of dollars in
advances.
And a new client at the time for him, who’s now become his main
client, Soundview Publications down in Atlanta. And so these
conferences have much greater value than a lot of people really
attribute to them. Another key to attending these kinds of
conferences, and this is something I’d encourage every younger
copywriter or beginning copywriter to do, and that’s they give you
an opportunity to meet other copywriters. And the magic that
happens at the conferences during like, for example, at the AWAI
boot camp, the peer review session, can happen for you every day
on the phone in terms of your copywriting. Find a partner. Find
somebody who’ll crit your copy for you and who’s copy you can
crit, and together you can learn and grow. That’s how Gary
Bencivenga got started.

-- 2 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 3 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 3 of 32
He was with David Ogilvy’s agency. Left there. Paired up with
Dan Rosenthal, who was also a young writer at the time, and the
two of them critted each other’s copy and sharpened each other.
And the result was that both of them became multi-million-dollar
copywriters. So those kind of pairings are really important. And
also at those kinds of events, don’t be afraid to approach and talk
to people who are more senior than you, senior copywriters.
Bob Bly: 	They absolutely will talk to you. When I was starting, and I was
22 or 23, I was a kid. I had no experience. I was a chemical
engineer. I went to one of these events, my first one. And
Ed McClain – most of the people on the call know who he is. He
recently passed away – was unbelievably gracious to me and spent
so much time with me.
And for nothing in return. I mean there was nothing I could give
him.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, it’s an incredible opportunity. Right now – I haven’t even
counted them all – I probably have 15 open assignments on my
desk from seven or eight different major mailers. I can’t possibly
get to all of that work. And just in the last 30 days, I’ve hired four
young copy cubs whose samples I liked. Their samples, most of
them weren’t even in the particular areas that I needed copy on.
And I think I mentioned this to you the other day, Bob. The
education that you get from AWAI and from other sources on how
to write copy is absolutely invaluable.
It’s like going to Harvard and getting your bachelors, your
master’s, and finally your M.D. and graduating medical school.
But after you leave there, you don’t just hang up a shingle and start
operating on people. You go into an internship where you work
under the auspices of a practicing physician. And in the
copywriting field, that can be an enormous advantage for you as
well, to take your training and use it to get a position with a senior
writer who needs help, who has more work than he can do, and
who can coach you on real-world projects. And serve an
internship of a few months to a year under that writer. When
you’re done then, what do you have? You have a year’s worth of
samples that you can show people and say, “Clayton Makepeace or
Carline Anglade-Cole or Parris Lampropolis and I wrote this
together.” And that is like just busting the door down at these
major mailers.

-- 3 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 4 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 4 of 32
Bob Bly: 	Now, the immediate question that’s raising in everyone’s mind is
that sounds great. I would like to intern for a senior copywriter.
But not all senior copywriters do this. Some do and some don’t.
How do I make that happen? Is that through the networking?
How do I make that connection?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, I think networking is making the connection. Beyond that,
more and more senior writers are beginning to do this.
Arthur Johnson I hear is going to be doing this over at
Phillips Financial. I’m doing it with Healthy Directions and
several other clients. Parris Lampropolis is doing it at Soundview.
And as word gets out –
Bob Bly: 	Steve Wexler does it, and he’s on the web. He does it for
nutritional supplements.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And as more and more writers do it and demonstrate how
profitable it can be for the senior, and how beneficial it is for the
copy cub, it’s going to – it’s something that’s really gaining
momentum right now. And the key is again that you come away
with a solid real-world experience writing copy, dealing with
clients, getting the research, and winding up with samples that you
can really use to fill your dance card in the months ahead.
Bob Bly: 	So I mean Donald Trump has publicized this concept widely used
that – you used the metaphor of an intern or a resident in medicine,
but it’s really being an apprentice, right?
Clayton Makepeace: 	That’s exactly what it is. And you have much less of a chance of
having Trump call up and tell you you’re fired –
Bob Bly: 	Yes. [Laughs]
Clayton Makepeace: 	[Laughs]
Bob Bly: 	And you use one term, just so everyone’s on the same page, that
people may not know. A piece of jargon. You said crit. Can you
explain what – that’s short for criticism, right?
Clayton Makepeace: 	That’s just short for –
Bob Bly: 	Or critique –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Critique. Some people think it’s short for criticism. That’s too
negative for me. A critique is both the negative and positive
aspects of the copy. Showing you where it’s weak, but also

-- 4 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 5 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 5 of 32
helping you strengthen those areas, and providing additional ideas
for headlines and text and sale points and so forth, that can really
take response to the moon on packages you’re working on. Also,
the aspect of dealing with clients is critical here because that’s a
whole art in itself, and something that you can only learn by doing.
Bob Bly: 	That’s terrific. Any other advice on how you can get more clients?
You talked about networking. You’ve talked about having
samples. You haven’t talked about the Internet. What do you
think? So I always get asked this. “Should I have a website?”
And a lot of top copywriters don’t have websites. Should new
copywriters put up websites?
Clayton Makepeace: 	You know, there’re a lot of things that top copywriters don’t do.
The first thing that I never did was to ask for testimonials from
clients. And about six – and with my history of dropping out of
sight, focusing on one client, not promoting myself across the
marketplace, or even letting people know who I was, you put up a
blog on your blog site where you said, “Who are the top
copywriters in America?” And I couldn’t help but notice –
Bob Bly: 	And I listed my suggestion, and you were one of ’em.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yes. Yes, you did. And I appreciate that, but in reading the
responses from your people, it was shocking to me [laughs] to
discover that so many of them had no clue as to who I was.
Bob Bly: 	And the people they name were more of the people who are active
promoters.
Clayton Makepeace: 	That’s right. And not all of the active promoters –
Bob Bly: 	Are top copywriters –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Are top copywriters [laughs] so getting your name out there’s very
important, and having a website is I think in this day and age
crucial. However, a major caveat here. In everything that you do,
when you’re searching for clients, it’s absolutely essential to
remember that every word of copy, every word that you write to a
client about introducing yourself or your past successes or why
they should hire you is sales copy.
Bob Bly: 	Yeah, people don’t realize their self-promotions are samples.

-- 5 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 6 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 6 of 32
Clayton Makepeace: 	That’s right. And you go to some of these websites and they are –
many of them are stiff. Many of them are stilted, written more like
a technical writer would write something rather than a sales
copywriter. So yes, by all means, do a website. You can throw
them up for a couple of hundred bucks these days, but make sure
that the copy on the website itself is representative of the power
that you can bring to your sales copy. Another thing to do is – I’ve
never done this – six months ago I was planning to launch the total
package, and so I sent out an email to clients that I had worked for
over the years. Some of them I hadn’t talked to in a decade or
longer, and I just asked them if they would be so kind as to write a
couple of paragraphs about what I had done for them, or the value
that I brought to their companies over the years, and I was
absolutely shocked.
Everybody was so nice. I didn’t think I’d get that many comments.
I was interrupting their busy days. Some of these people are
running $100 million, $200 million companies, and yet Tom
Phillips, Brian Kurtz, Martin Weiss, all of these people. Kim
Githler at The Money Show, all sent me these wonderful long five,
six, seven paragraph-long testimonials. Gary Bencivenga wrote –
my competitors wrote great testimonials. You did.
Bencivenga did. Rutz did. And so those testimonials then bring
instant incredibility to anything that I say about myself, whether on
a website or in an email or in a FedEx package, with samples that I
send to clients. So as soon as you have some successes, and I do it
now with every single client I have. When I have a successful
package, I ask for a testimonial.
Bob Bly: 	I do that too, and I post them on my website. And if people go to
my website, which is my last name – www.bly – B as in boy, L-Y
– dot.com. Click on testimonials, and you’ll see page after page
after page. And it’s not only the quality of them. The actual
quantity is important. If it’s more then people would wanna read,
that’s good ’cause they just get overwhelmed, and they say, “All
right, enough already.”
Clayton Makepeace: 	That’s right.
Bob Bly: 	And they say, “If this many people say this, this guy must be
good.” So that’s great. So we’re talking about how do we get lots
of clients. You’ve given some great techniques. Networking,
testimonials, which everyone should get, putting up a website,
copy cubbing or apprenticing or interning with a more experienced
copywriter. You also talked about the strategy last time. You say

-- 6 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 7 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 7 of 32
look for local companies that should be using direct marketing but
aren’t.
’Cause most of your clients that you work for are major direct
marketers. But if you’re a beginner, I think you can get some
really great samples, new great work, for companies that aren’t
direct marketers but might wanna do a sales letter. And I’ve done
a lot of that.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, absolutely.
Bob Bly: 	You did something like that you said with boilers or water heaters?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, and the compensation doesn’t always have to be financial on
these things. I mean, of course, you have to pay the bills. But
sometimes there’re companies who don’t wanna lay out cash but
will trade in-kind services in return for copy. And at one point in
the early ‘70s, I decided I wanted to learn how to fly. And I was
making a good enough income that I could just barely pay the bills,
but I certainly didn’t have $5,000.00 or $10,000.00 to pay for
flight instruction and then to rent airplanes and fly. And so I just
sent a letter out to the flight schools in Los Angeles and told them
– the letter just said, “Look, you want new clients? New flight
students and new charters for your aircraft?”
“I wanna learn how to fly. You don’t wanna pay for marketing. I
don’t wanna pay to learn how to fly. There’s a deal to be made
here.” And I sent that letter out on a Friday. Saturday morning I
got a phone call, and one of the best flight schools in L.A. had
written back – or had called me and said, “Don’t talk to anyone
else. Come over here right now and talk to us. We need you.”
And I walked out of there with a $20,000.00 credit on their books
and took my first flight lesson that day. Another writer, a good
friend of mine, Daniel Rosenthal, is famous for going into a
company that isn’t familiar with direct marketing or direct
response and how much money it can make them, and simply
saying, “Look. I’ve got an offer you can’t refuse. I wanna do a
promotion for you, and all I want you to do is give me 30% of
sales.” Thirty percent of sales. I know one promotion he did that
made him $4 million using that kind of a deal. He simply went to
a company and said, “Let me write a sales letter for you.”
I think if I remember right it was only eight pages long. It took
him a week or two to get it perfect. They mailed it out, and his
30% got him $4 million. So finding companies that aren’t using

-- 7 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 8 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 8 of 32
direct marketing or don’t fully understand the value of strong copy,
and then talking to them about hiring you on spec for a percentage
of sales is great, because it’s always easy for them to sign an
agreement saying that they’re gonna give you 30% of this huge
new number that just boggles their mind. It’s the easiest sale in the
world to make. You’re saying to them, “Look, I’m gonna give you
$9 million” – in Rosenthal’s case, he was saying, “I’m gonna give
you” – what’s the math on that?
Bob Bly: 	Twelve I guess.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Twelve million dollars if you –
Bob Bly: 	Or roughly –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah. “And of that, you’ve gotta pay me four.” And so all the
client heard was, “You’re gonna give me $8 million in the next
30 days?” [Laughs] And he did the deal.
Bob Bly: 	Well, that’s the old Jay Abraham when he teaches his principle of
selling. He says, “You go up to someone and say ‘Will you give
me a quarter if I’ll give you a dollar?’”
Clayton Makepeace: 	Um hm.
Bob Bly: 	Now, you mentioned going to companies that don’t use direct
marketing and then creating a huge success for them that they
wouldn’t have. But what about the idea of – I’ve heard this
suggested by amateurs. They say, “Why don’t I go look for direct
mail packages from direct marketers?” You know, companies,
newsletter publishers or whatever, seminar promoters, book
publishers, that I see – I see a lot of them that aren’t so good.
Should I approach them about saying, “Hey, your piece isn’t that
good. I can beat it.” And then get a job that way?
Does that work?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, that’s what I call being a direct response bully. We covered
that in pretty good detail on the call, and we paid special attention
in that section. It really is a great way. It helped me in my early
career, and it’s an offer that clients quite often just can’t refuse.
Bob Bly: 	Now, this all sounds well and good, but I’m introverted myself,
and I’m shy. And a lot of people have told me at copywriting boot
camps, seminars, “Why be very nervous or shy or intimidated
about contacting a potential client if I started as a freelancer?” I

-- 8 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 9 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 9 of 32
don’t know if you consider yourself an introvert or an extrovert,
but regardless, how do you do that?
How do you get over that?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, it just helps to understand that the people that hire writers are
essentially charged with a task of hiring writers, of finding great
new writers, and knowing that these people are just dying to find
the next hot copywriter for their company. The one missing
ingredient that they have is good sales copy. If they had more
writers, they could introduce more products. They could grow the
products that they have now more quickly. It’s the one thing they
can’t. It’s the one scarce resource for them. They can hire
editorial people to create editorial products until sundown.
They can find people to work in their company no problem.
Finding a copywriter who can sell their product is the one thing
they’re missing. And so I think just having the confidence of
knowing that these people’s personal success in life is tied to
finding you, gives you a level of confidence in talking to these
people that should push you over the top.
Bob Bly: 	Now, you’ve said in the previous call that the supply and demand
balance of copywriting to people who need copy is heavily
weighted in the favor of the supply, the copywriter. That’s
certainly true for you, but is this true for people who are less
experienced or some of the starting or novice copywriters on this
call?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, it’s true for everybody. The only question is who’s gonna
hire you? There’s no doubt that the supply demand equation is
tilted very heavily in the favor of the writer. This is a writer’s
market. Like in real estate, they say this is a seller’s market, right?
In the direct response industry, the entire industry is a copywriter’s
market. As I said before, they need us.
Bob Bly: 	I, by the way, would agree with this for people listening. I mean I
don’t have – Clayton mentioned that he had seven or eight jobs
that he’s got to assign out. I mean I turn away, and a lot of people
at AWAI have asked to be on my referral list ’cause I refer leads to
other people, but I turn away – and I’ve always told people this for
years – we turn away four to five assignments for every one we
take.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Oh, absolutely. And on the referral side, it’s hard to know who to
refer to because, for example, I called Kim Krause the other day. I

-- 9 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 10 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 10 of 32
wanted to get on her dance card for early next year. She doesn’t
have an open slot ’til July. But the thing here is that the very fact
that I’m hiring writers who are AWAI graduates, and in most cases
have nothing more than spec assignments to show, means that
there are opportunities out there. And also, as I said earlier, our
clients are just looking for some indication that you can do a good
job on the copy that you’re writing. It’s hard to believe.
I know a lot of our listeners may not believe this, but the fact of the
matter is that although there are hundreds more beginning writers
today than ever before, thanks to AWAI, and some of the things
that Bob and I have done, and others and Gary Halbert and John
Carlton and Gary Bencivenga and others are doing, that’s attracted
more young writers to the field. But of those young writers, I think
you’re going to find that they fall into several groups. Some have
a dream but will never quite take the bit in their mouth and go out
there and find clients. Others are easily discouraged, and the first
time or two that they go out to try to find clients and they’re unable
to get an assignment, they’ll just give up. And that’s a tragedy,
because you’ve got to work through those times. You’ve got to
learn how to deal with the rejection that comes along from time to
time with being a copywriter. It’s never nasty.
It’s never mean-spirited. But you spend all this time getting ready.
You send out your samples, and if somebody isn’t enthusiastic
about hiring you right off the bat, some people just get
discouraged. And that’s terrible, but it’s true. The people who
will succeed are the ones who have a reasonable expectation going
forward, understanding that they have the skills, the talents, the
training to get started. They need to spend some time doing
practical work in the real world, an internship or residency if you
wanna call it that, and pick up skills that way, make contacts,
network. Do all of these things.
The money will come. I’m living proof of it, and every other
copywriter that I’ve introduced you to in the total package and that
you’ve heard of, and Bob himself, will attest to the fact that as I
said at AWAI, it ain’t all a bed of roses. You have to be willing to
persevere. But if you do that, the money’s there. It will come.
Bob Bly: 	Well, it’s like in the movie A League of Their Own where Geena –
whatever her name is – Davis I think, complains to Tom Hanks.
She says, “But I don’t wanna do this.”
“It’s really hard.” He says, “Of course it’s hard. That’s the good
part. If it was easy, everyone would do it.”

-- 10 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 11 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 11 of 32
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah. There’s no crying in baseball.
Bob Bly: 	Yeah. There’s no crying in baseball.
Clayton Makepeace: 	[Laughs]
Bob Bly: 	Now, we’ve got a couple of emails coming in that I have some
good questions. First is, “I’ve signed up for your teleseminars.
I’m listening now. And in these calls, aren’t you guys – isn’t
Clayton mostly talking about just a small handful of clients –
Agora, Phillips, Boardroom – who pay royalties based on
head-to-head competition between top copywriters to beat a
control? Or is most of this information just as applicable to the
majority of copywriting clients?” So what say you?
Clayton Makepeace: 	I think we’ve touched on that.
Bob Bly: 	Yeah.
Clayton Makepeace: 	That the demand for copywriters is absolutely massive out there.
Everybody from your local drycleaner to your local car dealership
to your local shops need advertising. And whether it’s print ads,
direct mail, or Internet promotions, the one thing that’s restricting
their growth is lack of strong sales copy. And so just like I started
out writing for local fundraising organizations, local bakeries, local
manufacturers, local restaurants, there are opportunities in every
one of those areas. I live in the tiniest little town imaginable. We
have a company here that sells paint. We have a company here
that sells technology.
And we have a company here that sells packaging, boxes and
shipping cartons. Every one of those companies needs more
clients. They have the capacity to produce product. What they
don’t have is enough people calling them up and doing deals with
them to buy their product. That’s B to B, but it’s – people at work
are motivated by the same things that people at home are. They
wanna be successful.
Bob Bly: 	So you’re saying these guys can be reached, and instead of a flat
fee, you can offer them a Dan Rosenthal type deal where you’ll
say, “I’ll write this for you in exchange for some percentage of the
increase in sales.”
Clayton Makepeace: 	Absolutely. If you allow yourself to get locked into the flat fee
thing with these local companies, here’s what happens. They right

-- 11 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 12 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 12 of 32
now have the local cable guy, the local newspaper guy, the local
TV and radio guys banging on their doors and saying, “You need
to advertise. And if you buy time or you buy space from those
salespeople, they will write your ad for you free.”
Bob Bly: 	Um hm.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And that ad is exactly worth what you paid for it. It’s worth
nothing. And those ads don’t work for these people, and they try
them for a while and then they say, “Oh, advertising doesn’t work.
Marketing doesn’t work.” The problem wasn’t the medium. The
problem was the message. And so when you go into those places
and you offer them to come in and write something and give them
a fee of $2,000.00 to write an ad or $5,000.00 or $10,000.00 to
write a direct mail piece, their toes are gonna curl up.
Copy isn’t worth anything. I’m getting it free right now. Right?
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	So give it to ’em free. You know, give it to ’em free. Just say,
“Look, okay. Here’s what we’ll do. I’ll write this package for
you, and I want 10, 20, 30 – pick your number – percent of the net
sales from that ad.” Right?
Bob Bly: 	Um hm.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, to them, you just said I’m gonna write free and you’re not
gonna have to pay me anything ever because advertising doesn’t
work. So how can they turn that down? You’re offering to give
them a week or a month or two months of your time, whatever it
takes to get this done, and they don’t have to pay you up front, and
they’re probably not gonna have to pay you anything at all because
advertising doesn’t work as far as they’re concerned. So that’s a
tremendous opportunity. I did something like this with
Guthy Renker. They had hundreds of thousands of people who
had bought their products, including Vanna White’s teeth
whitening kit. Once they sold the main product, they never
contacted those people again because they had no frame of
reference on how direct mail works.
And so we structured a whole department in the company that did
nothing but direct mail promotions to people who had bought
Vanna’s products. And each sales letter was signed by Vanna, and
they got a monthly newsletter, and sales soared. So again, the key
is think about the frame of reference of your potential client. And

-- 12 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 13 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 13 of 32
then think about the easiest way to sell to him that will break you
out of that strict fee structure. As a copywriter, you’re a
salesperson. The most important product you’ll ever sell is
yourself. And so you need to spend time every month thinking
about the offers that you can make.
When you write for a product, the offer is a key component of your
promotion. When you’re writing for yourself, the same is true.
You need to think about the offer and structure offers that potential
clients find it difficult, if not impossible, to say no to.
Bob Bly: 	Now, we’re getting emails that are of two camps. The first camp –
and Julie, I want your feedback on this – the first camp is that,
“Gee, I’m sending my emails and I’ve sent several, and you guys
aren’t getting to them.” And the second camp is, “You guys are
starting to answer emails and you haven’t gotten through all the
agenda yet.”
Clayton Makepeace: 	[Laughs]
Bob Bly: 	[Laughs]
Julie McManus: 	Actually, I wanted to interrupt quickly too, because I am seeing a
common question here, and I’ve seen it over and over again for a
while now actually. People are really concerned about getting
ripped off. They wanna make sure that they know how they can
hold their clients accountable for what they are owed.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Good. I get that a lot –
Julie McManus: 	Talk about that a little bit? Yeah –
Bob Bly: 	Yeah, I get that question a lot. Before I answer it though, I wanna
say that we will cover all of the agenda items today on this call,
and should we for any reason fall short because we’re answering
current questions or emails or whatever, we will hold additional
agenda items over until the next call, and we will keep doing this
until the questions are answered.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right, so we will get to every agenda item on this call. If any are
left over, you will get ’em on the next call, and you will get every
agenda item we promised for all four calls, and we will answer
every question that we get. We may add a little more time at the
end to do that, and we won’t charge you extra, but you’ll get
everything.

-- 13 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 14 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 14 of 32
Bob Bly: 	Absolutely.
Julie McManus: 	And I’m actually seeing also that even though a specific question
with the person who’s answering it is not necessarily being asked,
the answers are – they’re being answered. So even if it doesn’t
seem like a question is being answered, a lot of the questions that
are coming through are actually being answered in your
conversation.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Great.
Bob Bly: 	Yeah, now let’s go back to that. How do you prevent getting
ripped off? So you make a deal with the local paint guy that you
described, and you say 10% of sales. And he mails you a letter and
you never hear from him.
Clayton Makepeace: 	The key there, of course, is trust. First off, I have always required
the clients that I do this deal with – and it’s not an unreasonable
request to make – simply because you’re putting your time on the
line and it’s costing the client very little or nothing. A very
reasonable request to make is to establish a benchmark for what
their advertising is doing for them now, and then to include you on
the daily reports or weekly reports, sales reports, showing what
your promotion is doing. Those reports should come directly from
their order department, and that will give you some sense of
assurance.
Bob Bly: 	And I can assure people that it is not worth the time and trouble for
them to cook a second set of books just for a copywriter. They’re
not gonna do it.
Clayton Makepeace: 	No, it’s not. And it exposes them to legal liability –
Bob Bly: 	Yeah –
Clayton Makepeace: 	As well.
Bob Bly: 	It’s not gonna happen.
Clayton Makepeace: 	There’s another factor there, too, you have to be aware of. Always
remember – when I said earlier, you are essential missing
ingredient. If you write a promotion for this company and it blows
the doors off everything they’ve ever done, and it brings in
millions of dollars or thousands of dollars they otherwise wouldn’t
have earned, do you think they’re going to do anything at all to risk
alienating you? At that point, it’s not a one-time job anymore.

-- 14 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 15 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 15 of 32
These people are going to do everything in their power to get you
to sign a long-term contract to make sure that you do this work for
them every month and continue producing these results. And I
have that very specific experience at Security Rare Coin, where I
wrote one monthly promotion on the IF - com to his house file
people, got him ten times the response he normally would get, and
as soon as the money started coming in, this guy was on the phone
to me offering to pay moving expenses, quarter-of-a-
million-dollar-a-year salary, a new house in Minneapolis, and 5%
of gross sales if I would just come there and build a direct
marketing department for him. So a person would be a fool to do
anything to make you question whether or not they’re paying you a
fair royalty.
Bob Bly: 	Now, we included sample written agreements in our course
materials if not, ’cause someone’s asked about – we’re talking
about the email I just got is “Talking about how to contact people
is great, but how do we secure the deal? What should a contract
look like? What should be included?” I’ve got actually on my
website, if you go to bly.com – don’t do it now but do it after the
call – and you click on the main home page, www.bly.com, you
click on a button called methodology, which explains how I work.
One of those menu choices that’ll pop up at the bottom is my
standard agreement. And I can’t give it to you as a legal contract
’cause I’m not a lawyer, but you can see what I use, and I think it’s
a pretty good standard agreement.
Clayton Makepeace: 	That’s great. And in preparation for this also, I decided something
I really haven’t mentioned to the redhead or Julie or you, and that
is that I have years’ worth of contracts in my files that I signed
with many clients, and they cover many different kinds of deals.
They cover flat feet plus royalty, advance plus royalty, overrides
on ongoing sales from new customers I generate, and what I wanna
do is I wanna take those contracts and black out the names of the
customers, or my clients, and the specific percentages, and just
give you rough guidelines on those. But that way, you can see the
actual deal point and the actual copy that lawyers were paid – the
contracts that lawyers were paid thousands of dollars to create.
And you can pattern contracts that you write on that. They’ll be
two kinds of situations. Three actually.
There’re clients that basically do business on a handshake deal. If
it’s an established company with a good reputation for paying
royalties or commissions or just paying your bills – to copywriters,
then you can usually feel pretty comfortable with that. The other
kind of deal is the deal where the customer, the client, has a

-- 15 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 16 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 16 of 32
standing contract. That will be in situations where it’s a company
that’s used to hiring writers often. In that situation, they have
standard contracts that they send out. And that can be a little
daunting because this is something that appears to be cast in stone.
You know, “This is how much we pay.”
“This is the royalty. This is when you get paid your royalty. And
these are the clauses for if you fail to perform” or blah, blah, blah,
right?
Bob Bly: 	Yep.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Every one of those points is negotiable. So don’t allow yourself to
just look at it, sign it, and send it back. Go through the points.
Think about each one, and pay attention to the minutia on those.
But anyway, I’m going to take those contracts and we’re gonna
create a .PDF of it –
Bob Bly: 	So we will get you a .PDF of real contracts, and you can see mine
immediately online –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yep –
Bob Bly: 	So that’ll answer that question. Now, we understand that we can
ask for royalties. But why would a client pay, or how could a
beginning copywriter ask for, the same royalty as that of a Parris or
Dan Rosenthal or Clayton get? Why would a client pay me as a
beginner top dollar?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, because the customer that you generate for him has the same
value to the client as a customer that I generate or Parris generates.
The royalty is the one part of the fee arrangement that should be
absolutely equal no matter who’s doing the work. One of these
days, clients who are really trying to get the best writers they
possibly can will standardize their deals. Instead of paying me
$50.00 a thousand and paying someone else $30.00 and then
getting away with paying somebody else $15.00, they’ll be a
standing royalty rate that everybody will be offered. The thing is
that your royalty, even at $50.00 a thousand, even if you were able
to get $50.00, which I usually do from my financial clients, it’s
only 10% of his cost of direct mail. Even if your royalty is 10% of
the net on Internet sales, it’s only 10% of the net. It’s the most
important component in this company’s growth, and yet it only
costs him 10%.

-- 16 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 17 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 17 of 32
So the royalty itself is chicken feed. And the fact is that the money
you produce for him, and the clients you produce for him, the new
customers you produce for him, have every bit as much value as
the money and the customers that I generate or anyone else does.
Julie McManus: 	Guys, I wanna read something that just came through because it’s
just really incredible, and it really brings home what you’re saying.
This is from Mark in Canada actually. It says, “I came in touch
with the AWAI copywriting course around 15 months ago and
studied your business model. In May of this year, I took the
plunge and quit my job to become a full-time independent
copywriter. The results: In June I only earned $3,000.00. I broke
even in July and August, and in September I earned $12,000.00.
This month I will make nearly $20,000.00, and I have work
booked up into January, and I haven’t even completed my
copywriting website yet.”
Bob Bly: 	I think I know who this is, so to my friend Mark K., we won’t
reveal your last name, congratulations. I’m proud of you.
Clayton Makepeace: 	That’s wonderful, but not a surprise. This is somebody who
learned his craft, and who then followed his dream and stuck with
it. And month one, three thousand bucks. Okay, fine. You know?
But now he’s almost seven times that amount and he’s booked up.
So that’s the reality of the situation. I –
Bob Bly: 	What if he – go ahead, Clayton –
Clayton Makepeace: 	I wanna make just one very brief other point about the royalty
situation. A royalty is a no-risk deal to the client. If you don’t
kick the living daylights out of his control, I don’t care if your deal
says you need $1,000.00 a thousand royalty, it’s not gonna cost
him a dime if you don’t be at his control. So allowing yourself to
be negotiated down on the royalty rates is just specious. It’s
ridiculous. There’s no reason to do it.
It’s easy to be intimidated into it, and if you are, and I have been
from time to time, that’s fine. But it’s you spending your own
money at that point.
Bob Bly: 	But what about you mentioned standard practices though. I called
one very well known direct marketer, or they called me rather a
couple years ago, and I asked about this. And there was a
statement, very terse, from the marketing director who’s a
wonderful person. She said, “We don’t pay royalties.” And that
was the end of the discussion.

-- 17 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 18 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 18 of 32
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, I know who that is.
Bob Bly: 	Yeah, I know you know.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And that was a blatant lie because they’d been paying me royalties
for about seven years at that point.
Bob Bly: 	Curse you Sally! [Laughs]
Clayton Makepeace: 	[Laughs] The fact of the matter is there are a couple of companies
that say that, but the fact of the matter is when it gets right down to
it, if they want to get on a writer’s dance card who charges
royalties, the only way to do that is to pay the royalty. A friend of
mine sent out an email earlier, probably about a month ago. Now
this friend has been in the copywriting area now for several years
and makes six figures. And at my suggestion, because the problem
was they were getting way too many requests for work than they
could possibly handle and potential clients were getting frustrated
because they weren’t able to get on the writer’s dance card. And
so I said, “Look, you can” – and it was becoming a distraction to
the writer. And so I just said, “Look, it’s very simple. All you do
is you send out an email.”
“You double your fees. You’re asking $15,000.00 right now in
advance, ask $30,000.00, and instead of $25.00 a thousand
royalties, ask $50.00. Okay? Then two things are gonna happen.
First of all, the volume of this pestering is going to stop, and
secondly, if you do get a client who calls and says, “Okay, fine,”
now you’ve doubled your income. So this writer who trusts me
implicitly – which may or may not be a mistake, I don’t know –
did exactly what I said that hour. Within an hour, I got a call from
one of the major mailers out there who said, “Do you know what
so-and-so’s doing?” And I said, “No, what?” And they told me,
and they were, “Oh, this is outrageous.”
I said, “Well, you know, they’re booked up. And what it comes
right down to is if you want their work, you’re gonna have to pay
their fee.” And long story short, this writer has since – who is
already booked up well into next year – has accepted two
assignments at double their normal rate. So it’s one of those
situations. Write great copy. Learn your craft, write great copy,
and then let your clients raise your fees for you.
Bob Bly: 	So obviously one way I can make each client more profitable for
me is just to double or just raise my rates. But you’ve got some

-- 18 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 19 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 19 of 32
other ways. How else can you make more money from the same
clients?
Clayton Makepeace: 	One of the things I learned early on – Bob King and I used to talk
about this – is that the time I’m most ready to write a package for a
product, to write a promotion for a given product, is five minutes
after I just delivered a package for that product. I usually will have
tons of ideas I wasn’t able to work into the text, and tons of copy
that I’ve had to clip out just so the thing would be the proper
length. And plus I’ve had my mind totally devoted to that product
for weeks, and I know that product inside out. I know the market
inside out. Writing about it is effortless at that point. The learning
curve has already been played out. So the best time for me to write
a second package for a product is right after I wrote the first one.
And so I quite often will tell a client – and Eric Buetel does this
too – I’ll often tell a client, “Look, yes, I want to write for this
product. I need to tell you a couple of things. Quite often, the first
package I write for a new product does well, but it’s not a home
run because it’s my first package for the product. Quite often, the
second package I write for a product blows the doors off
anything’s done before for all the reasons I just cited.” And so
instead of selling one at a time, I say, “I wanna do two packages in
rapid sequence for this product. The first one I’ll deliver on
such-and-such a day. And then two weeks later I’ll deliver you
first-draft copy for a second one.”
“I’ll charge you a $25,000.00 advance for the first one, and a
$12,500.00 advance for the second one.”
Bob Bly: 	Fifty percent of the first?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Fifty percent of the first.
Bob Bly: 	Which is what Don Mahoney, co-founder of AWAI, does. He uses
the same technique.
Clayton Makepeace: 	It doesn’t always work, but it works often enough that it’s worth
doing. It’s worth trying. And the fact that that second package is
out there gives you a – more than doubles your chances of getting
a control and getting royalties.
Bob Bly: 	Doubles your chances of getting a control and royalties, and
you’ve increased the upfront sale 50%?

-- 19 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 20 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 20 of 32
Clayton Makepeace: 	That’s right. Well, in my case, the upfront sale is up 75 because I
got full rate on the first package and half-rate on the second. So
another way to do it is, and this is difficult to do at the very
beginning but once your schedule starts filling up, and as we just
heard from your friend in Canada, it can happen fairly quickly, in a
matter of months, you can simply say to the client, “I’m booking
my schedule for the next six months, and I only have four slots
left. How many do you want?” And more often than not, the
client will come back and say, “I want two” or “I want three” or “I
want four.” And all of a sudden, you’re getting $30,000.00,
$40,000.00, $50,000.00 in advances this month for packages – for
work you won’t have to do for several months.
Bob Bly: 	And that’s a great script, and people should use it exactly as you
said it. “I’m booking my schedule for the next six months. I have
four slots left. How many do you want?” Not “Do you want
one?” but “How many do you want?” You never wanna ask a yes
or no question so –
Clayton Makepeace: 	I said that once. Wendy will remember when. It was in the ‘90s,
early ‘90s if I remember right. With Boardroom, I had just written
my first package for them. It was the first magalog they had ever
done, and it was for a product called the Big Black Book, which
was an old product that had been around forever, and the response
in the mail to that product was steadily declining. They were about
to retire the product permanently and they said, “Well, what the
heck. We got this writer we’ve never used before,
Clayton Makepeace.”
“He wants to do something we’ve never done before, which is a
magalog. What do we have to lose? Let’s have him try this
product out.” I wrote the copy. We mailed it. The response came
in at as high as it was when the book was brand new. It completely
revitalized the product.
Instead of having to invent a new product to grow their sales, they
had an old product now that was suddenly new again. They were
absolutely thrilled. They just could hardly sit still. I sent them an
email saying, “Great. I’m really happy that the package worked
well. I’m scheduling up the rest of my year. I need to tell you that
I’ve already filled four slots and I’m getting calls from customers
every single day wanting more copy.”
“But based on this success and the amount of money I’m going to
make on this with you, I would really prefer to give Boardroom
first shot at these packages. So if I hear from you in the next 24

-- 20 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 21 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 21 of 32
hours, we’ll set that up. If I don’t, then I’m going to go ahead and
send out my customary email to the rest of my clients offering
them these slots for the rest of the year.” The next day, Boardroom
called, took every slot I had available for the year, which was eight
slots. And cut me a check for almost $100,000.00 in advances,
which is 50% of the $25,000.00 that I charge. I collect – and this
is important – when you’re invoicing for advances, let’s say your
advance is $25,000.00.
Typically what the client will wanna do is pay you half of that
advance when you begin the work, and half of that advance when
you deliver acceptable final copy. I don’t like doing it that way
because too many times over the years I’ve had customers say,
“Yeah, I want you in April.” And April comes along and there’s
no research kit, or they’ve cancelled the product, or the premium
that’s supposed to drive the package is proven to be a dud, and so
they have no work for you and you’re sitting there with nothing to
do in the month of April. So what I require is that my clients pay
half of my $25,000.00 advance the minute they schedule the work.
Bob Bly: 	That’s what we do. We say the slot we’ve promised is not held
until the deposit is received.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Um hm. Absolutely right. And then that 50% of your total
advance is also in – I’ll show you this in the contracts you’ll
receive – is also a kill fee.
Bob Bly: 	It’s nonrefundable.
Clayton Makepeace: 	It’s nonrefundable. If you and the client can’t work together, if
you can’t get along, if the chemistry is wrong –
Bob Bly: 	If they cancel the project. It never happens –
Clayton Makepeace: 	They cancel the project, if they’re not ready with research on time,
if they don’t respond with crits to the copy in a timely manner, you
simply resign the assignment, keep the $12,500.00, and walk away.
Right? That almost never happens because if that clause is in the
contract, the client’s gonna make darn sure that he’s got everything
you need to get you going on time. So that’s very important. On
the other end of the cycle, too, when you’re dealing with clients on
onesies and twosies collecting the second half of the advance when
the copy is final is fine. But if you’ve got a long-term relationship
with a client, they know that you write killer copy and you’ve had
some successes with them, I demand payment of the second half of
the advance when I submit first-draft copy. The client knows that

-- 21 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 22 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 22 of 32
I’m motivated to get the copy final because I don’t collect royalties
’til it’s final.
And so I have plenty of incentive to not let the thing ride after I
deliver first draft. But there’s no reason at that point really not to
collect the money for the second half of the advance as soon your
first-draft copy is submitted.
Bob Bly: 	So in the contract, you have to be very specific about the payment
terms: when you get paid, the invoices, etc. What else should I
look for or worry about in my copywriting contract that you’re
gonna send me samples of?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right, well in the – as we ran for this call series, we talk about
landmines to avoid in copywriter contracts, and there are five
major ones and then a few minor ones. The first one is ownership
of the copy. Some marketing companies have sent me contracts
that say they own the copy. They can do any darn thing they want
with it as long as they want with it, and my compensation is tied
just to, for example, direct mail use of the copy. Ownership of the
copy is fine. Allowing the client to own the copy is fine, so long
as there’s also a clause in the contract that says that every use of
that copy will result in a payment to you. And then working
through with the client how the copy might be used.
You’re writing a direct mail package. Are they going to email it?
Are they going to turn it into a print ad? Are they going to excerpt
your copy for a television or radio script? Well, if so, then there
needs to be clauses in the contract that makes sure that you get
paid for every use of that copy. And the payment again for
non-direct mail use, where you’re going for quantity and you’re
royalty is tied to the number of pieces mailed, the compensation
agreement for television and radio and the Internet are quite
different. Because they’re cheaper media, and there’s a greater
likelihood that the client will make money using your copy in
those media.
And so in those situations, instead of going on a royalty per
thousand pieces mailed or per thousand impressions, I prefer to go
with a commission on net sales.
Bob Bly: 	So online – so what would that commission be let’s say for email
or web copy?
Clayton Makepeace: 	My standard is 10%.

-- 22 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 23 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 23 of 32
Bob Bly: 	Of net?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Um hm.
Bob Bly: 	Okay.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yep. And that – on many Internet promotions, for example, it’s
really very close to the growth.
Bob Bly: 	Yeah –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Because –
Bob Bly: 	Because no –
Clayton Makepeace: 	No marketing costs to speak of and they tend to deliver their
products online, so they don’t have to print and mail products. So
that’s very close. When I’m selling products such as supplements
or anything on television or radio, there is a hard cost to
fulfillment. You know, to shipping the product, and having the
product made and then shipping it, and the costs of promotion are
higher, and so of course, you have to examine what your net would
be per sale. And in cases where there’s a big question as to
whether the more profitable way would be to go with a royalty on
impressions or pieces mailed or a percentage of sales, I simply
have a clause in the contract that says compensation will be based
on the greater of the two formulas that follow. And the first one
will be a royalty formula and the second one will be a commission-
on-sales formula.
Bob Bly: 	So we’ve got the ownership of the copy, the compensation terms.
We’ve talked about the payment terms. What about penalty
clauses?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, I wanna get back to compensation terms –
Bob Bly: 	Okay –
Clayton Makepeace: 	’Cause there’re a couple of other things there. One of the tricks
that some mailers will use, for example, is that they will bury a
clause in the contract that says that you get no royalty on test lists.
I think that’s totally unfair. If they’re mailing to a test list, they
have an equal chance of making a bundle on that list or losing
money. In other words, the odds are they’re gonna break even
most of the time on those test lists. And so you deserve the same
royalty when they test a new file with your package as you do

-- 23 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 24 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 24 of 32
when they’re rolling out to other files. Believe it or not, there’s
some major mailers out there who will still insist on that, and it’s a
negotiating point.
I would stick by my guns as much as possible on that, because
there’s so many ways for that to be abused. They could do
massive test size on lists that they think are going to work, for
example, and not pay you for them. So it’s better just to have a
very clear simple thing. “Look, any time you mail it, I get the
royalty. Period.”
Bob Bly: 	So let me interrupt for a second, Julie. We have a large ambitious
agenda which, fortunately or unfortunately, we’re only about 60%
of the way through and it’s 1:00. And plus, we have a ton of email
questions that are piling up, some of which we’ve answered and
some of which we haven’t. I’m thinking if it’s okay now, I’m
thinking, Julie, we should continue today for another 15 minutes if
people are willing to stick with us. And if not, they can still hear it
right on MP3. It’s not like they’ll miss out.
Julie McManus: 	Yes.
Bob Bly: 	And we’ll tell them how to do that, or you’ll tell them how to do
that, and so my plan if everyone agrees is we’ll go another
15 minutes and we’ll continue at the pace we’re going ’cause this
is great information. At 1:15 we’ll wrap. Whatever we didn’t
cover on this agenda, that will be the beginning of our next call.
And whatever questions we didn’t answer will also get answered
during the next call and subsequent calls. So we will get through
all the agenda and we will answer all the questions. Does that
work for you guys?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, and I just wanna tell everyone that’s listening, please be
patient with us. We’re trying to give you as much information as
possible and as practical information as possible, and you have my
word that by the time this series is over, you will have had the
answers to everything we promised you and a lot, lot more.
Bob Bly: 	Yeah, don’t get panic-stricken. If you have something that you
wanna know and you feel uncomfortable ’cause we haven’t gotten
to it yet or it hasn’t been answered, email it to us and we are
keeping these all in a file and everything will be answered.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Okay, getting back to the contracts –
Bob Bly: 	Yes –

-- 24 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 25 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 25 of 32
Clayton Makepeace: 	Again, let me go through the other points quickly. Payment terms
are very important, not just how much, but when you get paid.
There’re companies – a lot of companies will say, “Well, we pay
once a quarter.” And a company just pitched me that kind of a
deal the other day and I wrote back with a twinkle in my eye and
just said, “Hey look guys, when I loan people money, I expect
interest. If you’re gonna sit on my royalty payments for three
months at a time and age them – I mean let’s not kid each other.
We know what you’re doing. You’re aging these royalty
payments.”
“If you’re going to age my royalty payments, I want interest.
Otherwise I want you to pay me monthly based on your mailing
receipts or even on your merge purge.” Merge purge – okay, a
merge purge is when a company rents many mailing lists. There
will be duplicate names on those lists, and so the company sends a
tape or a disk of all of the names that they’ve rented to a computer
house, or does it internally, and they run those lists against each
other and identify the duplicate names. Those names are then
removed from the most expensive list first to lower their costs of
mailing, and then from all of the other lists. So they end up with a
clean list with no duplicates, right? So at the time they end up with
a clean list, with no duplicates, they know exactly what their mail
quantity is going –
Bob Bly: 	And that’s what you’re paid on?
Clayton Makepeace: 	And that is – there is no reason to age your royalties one day past
the day that they know what the mail quantity will be. It may be
reasonable in some cases for them to say, “Yeah, but wait. We’ll
pay you the day we mail, and we’ll provide you with a postal
receipt showing you how many we mailed.” That’s fine too. But
this business of sitting on your royalty for three months is just their
way of nickel-and-diming you. It makes their books look good. It
makes their accountants look like they’re looking out for the
company.
It costs you money and disincentivizes you to work for that
company. So that’s something to look for. Penalty clauses in
contracts. Some contracts you’ll come across and they’ll say,
“Well, if you’re one day late we’re gonna charge you 10% of the
back-end payment,” or whatever. They’ll be some performance
guarantee tied to a timeline or a deadline. I avoid those at all terms
– in all cases. I will not accept that in a contract.

-- 25 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 26 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 26 of 32
Because that’s like walking up to a painter, a great painter, and
saying, “You have to paint a masterpiece in this many days and if
it takes you one day more, you’re gonna get paid – because you
wanna make it perfect, you’re gonna get paid less.” Jim Rutz said
once that the heartbreak of a blown deadline is quickly forgotten in
the warm glow of a hot new control.
Bob Bly: 	[Laughs]
Clayton Makepeace: 	The customer wants the hottest copy possible. You wanna give
them the hottest copy possible. Artificial deadlines are an obstacle
to doing that, and any penalty – any time they put in a clause that
penalizes you for trying to give them the hottest copy possible, is
counterproductive for both of you. So absolutely avoid those.
Bob Bly: 	Okay.
Clayton Makepeace: 	We already talked about kill fees. That’s a essential contract point.
Not every deal works out, even with established clients. Like I
said, the product may have proved to be a dud before you get to it.
There may be other problems, or they may just be so booked or so
busy they can’t get your research material to you on time. So if for
any reason the project doesn’t move ahead, make sure that that first
half of your advance is convertible to a kill fee. There are other
things you might look for, and I’ll include stuff on this in the extra
bonus, unadvertised bonus that everybody’s gonna get that
attended this call, that have to do with limiting the number of
drafts.
I have a client who is famous for diddling with a copy endlessly.
Bob Bly: 	And I know who he is, but we won’t mention his name.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right. And we both love him, but God bless his heart, I was
looking at some copy I delivered to him last year for a new project
and you know what the final draft number was? Twenty-seven.
Twenty-seven drafts of this package. You can’t do that and make
money. You have to basically say, “Look, I will give you three
drafts.” “I will give you five drafts.”
But there has to be a cut off. And then finally, there’s the legal
side of it, and I’m going – our next – two weeks from now the
article in the total package is going to be about the ethics and
legality of what we do, and how copywriters can stay safe. But it’s
very important to understand that we are in an increasingly
regulated environment, especially in the financial and health areas.

-- 26 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 27 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 27 of 32
And there is liability associated with creating promotions for these
areas. And so in every contract, I have a clause that says three
things. The first one is that the client alone is responsible for the
legality of the final copy that’s mailed. That means that every
statement of fact they will have to have substantiated and backed
up, and that the testimonials and appropriate disclaimers and all of
those things, they are responsible for putting into the copy.
I’m not a lawyer; I’m a copywriter. And so I make sure that the
client assumes responsibility for what is mailed. Secondly, if
somebody buys or uses their product and is harmed in any way, or
claims to be harmed in any way and sues the company and sues
me, two things happen. First, the company holds me harmless,
which means they cannot – the contract says they agree that they
cannot come back to me and sue me as part of that action. And
then the third thing is that should I be approached or incur any
costs because of a regulatory action, and again, they’ll be a lot
more on this in the total package in two weeks, that they indemnify
me. And that means that if I have to go to New York like I did one
year to the day before 9/11 and go up to the top of the World Trade
Center and testify before the Commodity Futures Trade
Commission, and hire an attorney and pay travel expenses to do all
of that, that the client has to reimburse me for those. So that gives
you about seven or eight key points for contracts.
And look for your .PDF on this because I think it’ll be very
helpful.
Bob Bly: 	Now, this is probably unnecessary, Julie, ’cause I think we all get
the total package, but just in case someone joined us and does not,
how do you sign up?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Julie’s not on.
Bob Bly: 	Julie’s not on –
Julie McManus: 	Sorry, sorry, I was muted. I don’t think anybody wants to hear my
dog barking in the background.
Bob Bly: 	I wanted to hear it but –
Julie McManus: 	[Laughs]
Bob Bly: 	We were asking – we were saying everyone gets the total package,
but just in case, so they make sure they don’t miss this contract

-- 27 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 28 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 28 of 32
article that Clayton is doing, where do we sign up if we’re not
getting it?
Julie McManus: 	www.MakepeaceTotalPackage.com.
Bob Bly: 	Terrific. All right. So we’ve covered the things to look for in a
contract, and we’re gonna get more on that. We’re gonna get a
contract guide and an article on the legal issues in two weeks in the
total package. I wanna come back to something that I think we
missed. We were talking about how to make each client more
profitable when we talked about raising your fees, and selling two
packages at the start, the second for 50% of the first, and booking
your schedule in advance. The one thing we left out though you
had mentioned is outsourcing, which in my first ten years of
copywriting, this was a totally alien concept to me.
But what do you outsource?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, I’m big on compartmentalizing. I have to do it when I’m
doing marketing consultation with my clients. And I go in and I
find that the president of the company is doing everything
including ordering toilet paper for the company.
Bob Bly: 	Um hm.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And micromanaging everything, and one of the first things I do is I
identify the unique things that that person brings to the job that
nobody else can do, or that that person does better than everybody
else. And then I identify the other things that they do, and the level
of person in terms of salary that could be hired to do those things,
or that maybe he already has on staff who could do those other
things. And then I prohibit him whenever possible from doing
things that he’s not the very best at ’cause frankly there’re other
people who do those things better than he does. And so the same
is true in my business as a copywriter. Early on, I asked myself the
question, “What is it that my customers are really hiring me for?
What do they want from me?” Well, they want hot sales copy.
And I do that better I think than anyone they could possibly hire.
You have to feel that way about yourself. But I feel I do that better
than anyone in the world. So they can’t do better than to hire me
to write copy. But you know what? I’m the world’s worst
researcher. Now, when I’m writing copy I’m making my clients
lots and lots and lots of money, and I’m making myself lots of
money.

-- 28 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 29 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 29 of 32
But when I’m researching, I’m doing a worst job than they could
pay somebody a lot less to do and get better research. And so early
on, I started hiring people like Dave Tomsick and Ray Knight and
other people and simply said, “Okay, look. This month I’ve got a
package on this,” or “Next month I have a package on this. Here’s
$2,000.00. I want you to give me a full research pack, 50, 60
pages, that answers all of these questions.” And the person would
go away and when I was ready to start my job, there I was. I had
all of the research I needed.
Later on – and that was in the days when I wasn’t really kind of in
the driver’s seat with clients, so I wasn’t able to say, “Look. I’ll do
this, but you guys have to give me the research.” And so I figured
it was cheaper for me to pay $2,000.00 to somebody else to do this
research for me on a package, than it was for me to spend 50% of
my time writing the package, which was $15,000.00 or $20,000.00
for that month, to do the research myself, and I’d end up with a
better product. Another thing that I did. When I write packages,
for example, for a Boardroom book, there’s a lot of typing
involved. They send you a hard copy book that could be four, five,
six hundred pages long. You have to read this whole thing and one
of the parts of the process is to find the information in the book
that is the forehead slapping stuff.
That says, “My God, I never thought of that. That’s brilliant.”
And then use that as proof elements in your copy. Well, that
requires a heck of a lot of typing. Again, I don’t get paid to type. I
get paid to write. I have fortunately these days, a person on staff
who’s a very quick typer and does that for me.
I simply read through the book, put a Post-It on the pages where I
want something taken verbatim out of the book, and highlight it,
and have somebody else do that typing.
Bob Bly: 	My assistant does that for me too, but we actually use an optical
scanner.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah. With an optical scanner, these days – we tried that.
[Laughs]
Bob Bly: 	These days it works.
Clayton Makepeace: 	These days it actually works, that’s right. So the principle here
though, no matter what kind of copy you’re going to write, is to
identify the things that make you the most money and that you do
better than anyone else, and that make your client the most money.

-- 29 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 30 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 30 of 32
And then identify the things that make you a lot less, and outsource
those things, either to the client, requiring that they provide this
material, or to people that you pay to do this work for you. You’ll
come out miles ahead. You’ll get the work done faster and it’ll be
better quality work.
Bob Bly: 	I always tell people this, and especially beginners, but I tell people,
not just copywriters, in any field this philosophy. They may look
at me like I’m an alien. And I say, “The best way I can explain it.
I haven’t been to the post office in 12 years. I haven’t been to the
bank in 15 years. Why do that when you can hire some bright high
school or homemaker or college student or professional for $10.00
an hour?”
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right –
Bob Bly: 	Or whatever it costs in your town.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right.
Bob Bly: 	And I have the best Internet researchers in the world for $20.00 an
hour. You can’t beat that.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Absolutely. Absolutely.
Bob Bly: 	So we’re on 1:15, so we’re gonna have to close. But let me deal
with one important issue a couple of people have emailed about,
and then we’ll save the rest of the points and emails for the first
half of our next call. And the question is this. We’ve talked about
that some copywriters are worried that the client will cheat them
out of a royalty, and you gave some very good advice about first
doing it with clients you can trust, but also asking for the mailing
receipts, and you explained how that worked. But what if you’re
still nervous or you’re not getting the mailing receipts? What if
you still think somehow “It doesn’t seem like I’m getting what I
should be getting?” Is there anything else you can do at this point?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, there’s not a lot. I mean No. 1, ask for mailing receipts, or
actual accounting if you’re working on commissions. Ask for
accounting report. The minute they issue those, they’ve made a
statement as part of the contract and they’re liable to it. Secondly,
understand that it’s an – and make it clear to them that it’s in their
best interest to overpay you if anything because if what you do
works for them, then the only way to keep you on board and keep
that money coming in is to keep you happy. Another way to do it
is to go ahead, and if it’s an information product for example, go

-- 30 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	2_How_to_get_Every_Penny_You_Are_Worth 	Page 31 of 32
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 31 of 32
ahead and subscribe to it. Get on their mailing list, and subscribe
to other information products, and then watch your mailbox.
If somebody tells – and this happened to me with a major big-name
client that everybody would recognize, who I worked for exactly
once. I wrote a promotion, and they said, “Well, it worked all
right. We’ll probably mail some.” And the next month I got one
in my mailbox, and the next month after that I got two or three, and
the month after that I got five or six. And I’m calling them up and
I’m saying, “Okay, guys. How many have you mailed?” “Oh,
well, we haven’t earned out yet.”
“The royalties still don’t equal the advance that we paid you.”
Well, that was total baloney, and as a result, I turned down every
future assignment they offered me. And basically they just
outsmarted themselves. I lost a little bit of money, but they lost a
hot copywriter who could’ve radically grown their business. And
as a result, they ended up really on the ropes. They’re still in
existence, but they’re a fraction of the size they were before.
Finally, if you’re suspicious and if you really think that something
funny is going on, call around.
In fact, it might not even be a bad idea to do this before you accept
the assignment, but talk to other writers. This is another place
where networking comes into play and where it’s so important.
Talk to other writers. See what kind of experiences they’ve had
with this client. If there’s been any foul play at all, the writer will
be more than happy to tell you, “Whoa. They screwed me big
time.”
Bob Bly: 	Absolutely. And news gets around. So on that note, Julie, what’s
our schedule for our next call?
Julie McManus: 	We are next Wednesday. Same time. Same bat cave. That would
be Wednesday, October 26th at 12:00 noon Eastern Standard Time.
Same call-in phone number, and same call-in passcode.
Bob Bly: 	And we thank you for being with us. And Clayton, Julie, thanks as
usual.
Julie McManus: 	Thank you.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Thank you guys. Write and let us know how we did.
Bob Bly: 	Yes, and we will get – and if you have more questions, send them
and they will all be answered.
